title: What is Text Classification?
concept: Text classification assigns predefined categories to a document, powering systems like spam filters and topic labelers.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of text classification?
exercises.explain: Text classification is the process of grouping text into predefined categories, such as sorting emails into spam and non-spam folders.

cards.title: The Inbox Detective
cards.body: How does your email inbox know exactly which messages are junk before you even open them? It's not magic—it's a smart AI assistant sorting the mail behind the scenes!

cards.title: Sorting Words Automatically
cards.body: Text classification is an AI process that reads a piece of text and automatically assigns it a label or category from a predefined list. It helps computers organize and make sense of massive amounts of written information instantly.

cards.title: The Mail sorter
cards.body: Think of text classification like sorting mail. Instead of reading every letter in detail, you quickly look at the contents and drop it into a specific bucket: "Bills", "Personal letters", or "Junk mail". Text classification does the exact same thing with digital text!

cards.title: Spam or Ham?
cards.body: When an email arrives, your email provider uses text classification to scan the message. If it detects words like 'free prize' or 'click here now', it labels the email as 'Spam' and hides it from your main inbox.

cards.title: Do They Really 'Understand'?
cards.body: People often think AI text classifiers actually 'understand' human emotions and stories the way we do. In reality, they look for mathematical patterns, common phrases, and word frequencies to make their best guess.

cards.title: From Text to Tag
cards.body: Text classification takes unstructured, messy text and maps it directly to clean, structured categories that we can easily filter and search.

left.text: "I absolutely loved the pizza, it was hot and delicious!"

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a real-world example of text classification?

options.text: Translating a French news article into German
Labeling a news article as 'Sports' or 'Finance'
Writing an automatic summary of a long news article
Reading an article aloud using a robotic voice

exercises.explain: Sorting news articles into categories like 'Sports', 'World News', and 'Finance' is a classic text classification task because it assigns a predefined label to the text.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A text classifier sorts text into a set of categories that have been defined beforehand.
exercises.explain: Text classification requires a predefined set of categories (like "Spam" and "Not Spam") to place the text into. It doesn't just invent new categories on the fly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how text classification works.
exercises.sentence: Text classification automatically assigns text to one or more predefined ______.
exercises.explain: Text classification relies on a predefined list of categories or labels (like "Sports", "Tech", or "Spam") to organize incoming documents.

exercises.prompt: An AI is trained to classify customer reviews into 'Positive' or 'Negative'. Which review will it classify as 'Negative'?

options.text: Review A: "The camera is amazing and takes beautiful photos!"
Review B: "I love this phone, best purchase I have made this year."
Review C: "The battery dies in ten minutes and the screen is cracked."

exercises.explain: Review C is an example of sentiment analysis (a type of text classification) where the AI classifies the text as 'Negative'. Reviews A and B are positive comments.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes the core task of a text classification system?

options.text: Assigning a predefined label or category to a piece of text
Generating an entirely new story from a short prompt
Translating a document from English to Spanish
Correcting grammar and spelling mistakes in an essay

exercises.explain: An AI text classifier reads the input text and assigns it to one or more predefined categories, such as sorting a movie review into 'positive' or 'negative'. Text generation, summarization, and translation serve different purposes.
